title: 4.5V以上高压锂电池机制与稳定性研究
objective: 从高质量文献中识别高电压运行下正极结构退化、界面副反应、氧释放、电解液氧化、容量衰减和安全性之间的机制缺口，生成可验证且可修订的研究假设。

papergraph.title: High-Concentration Ether Electrolytes for Stable High-Voltage Lithium Metal Batteries
papergraph.citation: Xiaodi Ren et al. (2019) High-Concentration Ether Electrolytes for Stable High-Voltage Lithium Metal Batteries doi:10.1021/ACSENERGYLETT.9B00381
papergraph.abstract: High-voltage (>4.3 V) rechargeable lithium (Li) metal batteries (LMBs) face huge obstacles due to the high reactivity of Li metal with traditional electrolytes. Despite their good stability with Li metal, conventional ether-based electrolytes are typically used only in <4.0 V LMBs because of their limited oxidation stability. Here we report high-concentration ether electrolytes that can induce the formation of a unique cathode electrolyte interphase via the synergy between the salt and the ether solvent, which effectively stabilizes the catalytically active cathodes and preserves their structural integrity under high voltages. Eventually, LMBs can retain 92% capacity after 500 cycles at 4.3 V with very limited Li consumption. More importantly, such ether electrolytes enable stable battery cycling not only under voltages as high as 4.5 V but also on highly demanding Ni-rich layered cathodes. These findings significantly expand knowledge of ether electrolytes and provide new perspectives of electrolyte design for high-voltage LMBs.
papergraph.benchmark: capacity retention, Li consumption, stable battery cycling
papergraph.contribution: development of high-concentration ether electrolytes that stabilize cathodes and preserve structural integrity under high voltages, enabling stable battery cycling at up to 4.5 V and on Ni-rich layered cathodes
papergraph.limitation: Here we report high-concentration ether electrolytes that can induce the formation of a unique cathode electrolyte interphase via the synergy between the salt and the ether solvent, which effectively stabilizes the catalytically active cathodes and preserves their structural integrity under high voltages.

papergraph.title: Molecular anchoring of free solvents for high-voltage and high-safety lithium metal batteries
papergraph.citation: Zhuangzhuang Cui et al. (2024) Molecular anchoring of free solvents for high-voltage and high-safety lithium metal batteries doi:10.1038/s41467-024-46186-y
papergraph.abstract: Constraining the electrochemical reactivity of free solvent molecules is pivotal for developing high-voltage lithium metal batteries, especially for ether solvents with high Li metal compatibility but low oxidation stability ( <4.0 V vs Li+/Li). The typical high concentration electrolyte approach relies on nearly saturated Li+ coordination to ether molecules, which is confronted with severe side reactions under high voltages ( >4.4 V) and extensive exothermic reactions between Li metal and reactive anions. Herein, we propose a molecular anchoring approach to restrict the interfacial reactivity of free ether solvents in diluted electrolytes. The hydrogen-bonding interactions from the anchoring solvent effectively suppress excessive ether side reactions and enhances the stability of nickel rich cathodes at 4.7 V, despite the extremely low Li+/ether molar ratio (1:9) and the absence of typical anion-derived interphase. Furthermore, the exothermic processes under thermal abuse conditions are mitigated due to the reduced reactivity of anions, which effectively postpones the battery thermal runaway. Advanced electrolyte is essential for high-energy-density lithium metal batteries. Here, the authors design a molecular anchoring dilute electrolyte via intermolecular hydrogen bonding with free solvents to improve the battery electrochemical and thermal stabilities.

papergraph.title: Lithium hexamethyldisilazide as electrolyte additive for efficient cycling of high-voltage non-aqueous lithium metal batteries
papergraph.citation: Danfeng Zhang et al. (2022) Lithium hexamethyldisilazide as electrolyte additive for efficient cycling of high-voltage non-aqueous lithium metal batteries doi:10.1038/s41467-022-34717-4
papergraph.abstract: High-voltage lithium metal batteries suffer from poor cycling stability caused by the detrimental effect on the cathode of the water moisture present in the non-aqueous liquid electrolyte solution, especially at high operating temperatures (e.g., ≥60 °C). To circumvent this issue, here we report lithium hexamethyldisilazide (LiHMDS) as an electrolyte additive. We demonstrate that the addition of a 0.6 wt% of LiHMDS in a typical fluorine-containing carbonate-based non-aqueous electrolyte solution enables a stable Li||LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 (NCM811) coin cell operation up to 1000 or 500 cycles applying a high cut-off cell voltage of 4.5 V in the 25 °C−60 °C temperature range. The LiHMDS acts as a scavenger for hydrofluoric acid and water and facilitates the formation of an (electro)chemical robust cathode|electrolyte interphase (CEI). The LiHMDS-derived CEI prevents the Ni dissolution of NCM811, mitigates the irreversible phase transformation from layered structure to rock-salt phase and suppresses the side reactions with the electrolyte solution. High-voltage non-aqueous lithium metal batteries suffer from poor cycling stability due to the presence of impurities in the electrolyte solution. Here, the authors report lithium hexamethyldisilazide to scavenge HF and H2O, prevent the Ni dissolution and suppress side reactions during cycling.

papergraph.benchmark: cycle life

papergraph.title: Enabling High-Voltage Lithium-Metal Batteries under Practical Conditions
papergraph.citation: Xiaodi Ren et al. (2019) Enabling High-Voltage Lithium-Metal Batteries under Practical Conditions doi:10.1016/J.JOULE.2019.05.006
papergraph.abstract: Summary Rechargeable lithium (Li)-metal batteries (LMBs) offer a great opportunity for applications needing high-energy-density battery systems. However, rare progress has been demonstrated so far under practical conditions, including high voltage, high-loading cathode, thin Li anode, and lean electrolyte. Here, in opposition to common wisdom, we report an ether-based localized high-concentration electrolyte that can greatly enhance the stability of a Ni-rich LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 (NMC811) cathode under 4.4 and 4.5 V with an effective protection interphase enriched in LiF. This effect, in combination with the superior Li stability in this electrolyte, enables dramatically improved cycling performances of Li||NMC811 batteries under highly challenging conditions. The LMBs can retain over 80% capacity in 150 stable cycles with extremely limited amounts of the Li anode and electrolyte. The findings in this work point out a very promising strategy to develop practical high-energy LMBs.

papergraph.title: Dynamic doping and interphase stabilization for cobalt-free and high-voltage Lithium metal batteries
papergraph.citation: Ziqing Yao et al. (2025) Dynamic doping and interphase stabilization for cobalt-free and high-voltage Lithium metal batteries doi:10.1038/s41467-025-58110-z
papergraph.abstract: Cobalt-free spinel LiNi0.5Mn1.5O4 (LNMO) positive electrodes, promise high energy density when coupled with lithium negative electrodes, due to the high discharge voltage platform. However, the intrinsic dissolution of Mn in positive electrode, electrolyte decomposition at high voltage, and dendrite growth on lithium severely compromise cycling stability, limiting the practical application. Herein, we propose ferrocene hexafluorophosphate as an electrolyte additive to achieve dynamic doping of Fe3+ in positive electrodes during electrochemical cycling. Furthermore, additive molecule preferentially decomposes at both the positive and negative electrode interfaces, forming thin, dense inorganic positive electrode electrolyte interphase and F, P-rich inorganic solid electrolyte interphase respectively, effectively stabilizing electrode interfaces. Consequently, the Li | |LNMO batteries based on modified electrolytes effectively enhance cycling stability and rate performance at a charge cutoff voltage of 4.9 V and an LNMO pouch cell performs consistently over 160 cycles. Additionally, the efficacy of ferrocene hexafluorophosphate extends beyond LNMO, demonstrating its universal applicability in stabilizing positive electrodes operating at challenging voltages, including LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2, LiNi0.6Co0.2Mn0.2O2, and LiCoO2 and a 470 Wh kg−1 level Li metal pouch cell was successfully realized. Cobalt-free Mn-based lithium metal batteries suffer from serious Mn dissolution and lithium dendrite problems. Here, authors propose ferrocene hexafluorophosphate as an electrolyte additive to achieve dynamic doping of positive electrode and interphase stabilization of electrodes.

papergraph.title: In Situ Catalytic Polymerization of a Highly Homogeneous PDOL Composite Electrolyte for Long‐Cycle High‐Voltage Solid‐State Lithium Batteries
papergraph.citation: Hua Yang et al. (2022) In Situ Catalytic Polymerization of a Highly Homogeneous PDOL Composite Electrolyte for Long‐Cycle High‐Voltage Solid‐State Lithium Batteries doi:10.1002/aenm.202201762
papergraph.abstract: High energy density solid‐state lithium batteries require good ionic conductive solid electrolytes (SE) and stable matching with high‐voltage electrode materials. Here, a highly homogeneous poly(1,3‐dioxolane) composite solid electrolyte (CSE) membrane that can satisfy the above‐mentioned requirements by in situ catalytic polymerization effect of yttria stabilized zirconia (YSZ) nanoparticles on the polymerization of 1,3‐dioxolane (DOL), is reported. The well‐dispersed YSZ nanoparticle catalyst leads to the polymerization conversion of DOL monomers up to 98.5%, which enlarges its electrochemical window exceeding 4.9 V. YSZ also significantly improves the room temperature ionic conductivity (2.75 × 10−4 S cm−1) and enhances the cycle life of lithium metal anode. Based on this CSE, the Li(Ni0.6Co0.2Mn0.2)O2 (NCM622)‐based solid‐state lithium battery shows a long cycle life over 800 cycles. This investigation encourages polymer SE toward practical high energy solid‐state batteries.

papergraph.title: Non-corrosive asymmetric fluorinated aryl sulfonimide lithium salt for high-temperature and high-voltage lithium metal batteries
papergraph.citation: Zhi Liu et al. (2025) Non-corrosive asymmetric fluorinated aryl sulfonimide lithium salt for high-temperature and high-voltage lithium metal batteries doi:10.1038/s41467-025-64737-9
papergraph.abstract: Electrolyte for high-temperature and high-voltage lithium metal batteries face challenges of thermally decomposition of lithium salt and interfacial corrosiveness with aluminum current collectors/cathode materials. Herein, we report a non-corrosive asymmetric lithium salt, i.e., lithium fluorinated aryl sulfonimide (LiFAS). Due to the fluorinated aryl substituent on the bis-sulfonylimide anion, the LiFAS exhibits several desirable physiochemical properties for high-temperature and high-voltage applications, i.e. high thermal stability (decomposition temperature ~388 °C), high voltage tolerance (anodic decomposition potential ~5.5 V vs. Li/Li+), and a high Li+ transference number of 0.62. Moreover, the LiFAS is able to efficiently inhibit the notorious Al-corrosion issue by forming a dense Al(FAS)3/AlF3 passivation layer on the surface of Al current collector. In addition, LiFAS could also promote the formation of inorganic-rich interphases on the cathode and anode. The unique advantages of LiFAS endow Li||NCM811 full cells great cycling stability and capacity retention at harsh cycling conditions (81% after 230 cycles at 60 °C and 4.5 V, 0.5 C charge/1 C discharge). This work inspires molecular engineering strategy for designing functional lithium salts to enhance the cycle life of LMBs under high-temperatures and high-voltages. Lithium metal batteries face interface stability issues at high temperatures and high voltage. Here, the authors develop a fluorinated lithium salt that resists corrosion, stabilizes interfaces, and enables long-term battery performance under harsh conditions.

papergraph.title: Advanced Electrolytes for Fast‐Charging High‐Voltage Lithium‐Ion Batteries in Wide‐Temperature Range
papergraph.citation: Xianhui Zhang et al. (2020) Advanced Electrolytes for Fast‐Charging High‐Voltage Lithium‐Ion Batteries in Wide‐Temperature Range doi:10.1002/aenm.202000368
papergraph.abstract: LiNixMnyCo1−x−yO2 (NMC) cathode materials with Ni ≥ 0.8 have attracted great interest for high energy‐density lithium‐ion batteries (LIBs) but their practical applications under high charge voltages (e.g., 4.4 V and above) still face significant challenges due to severe capacity fading by the unstable cathode/electrolyte interface. Here, an advanced electrolyte is developed that has a high oxidation potential over 4.9 V and enables NMC811‐based LIBs to achieve excellent cycling stability in 2.5–4.4 V at room temperature and 60 °C, good rate capabilities under fast charging and discharging up to 3C rate (1C = 2.8 mA cm−2), and superior low‐temperature discharge performance down to −30 °C with a capacity retention of 85.6% at C/5 rate. It is also demonstrated that the electrode/electrolyte interfaces, not the electrolyte conductivity and viscosity, govern the LIB performance. This work sheds light on a very promising strategy to develop new electrolytes for fast‐charging high‐energy LIBs in a wide‐temperature range.

papergraph.title: Quasi‐Localized High‐Concentration Electrolytes for High‐Voltage Lithium Metal Batteries
papergraph.citation: Wenlong Cai et al. (2023) Quasi‐Localized High‐Concentration Electrolytes for High‐Voltage Lithium Metal Batteries doi:10.1002/aenm.202301396
papergraph.abstract: The poor compatibility with Li metal and electrolyte oxidation stability preclude the utilization of commercial ester‐based electrolytes for high‐voltage lithium metal batteries. This work proposes a quasi‐localized high‐concentration electrolyte (q‐LHCE) by partially replacing solvents in conventional LiPF6 based carbonated electrolyte with fluorinated analogs (fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC), 2,2,2‐trifluoroethyl methyl carbonate (FEMC)) with weakly‐solvating ability. The q‐LHCE enables the formation of an anion‐rich solvation sheath, which functions like LHCE but differs in the partial participation of weakly‐solvating cosolvent in the solvation structure. With this optimized electrolyte, inorganic‐dominated solid electrolyte interphases are achieved on both the cathode and anode, leading to uniform Li deposition, suppressed electrolyte decomposition and cathode deterioration. Consequently, q‐LHCE supports stable cycling of Li | LiCoO2 (≈3.5 mAh cm−2) cells at 4.5 V under the whole climate range (from −20 to 45 °C) with limited Li consumption. A practical ampere‐hour level graphite | LiCoO2 pouch cell at 4.5 V and aggressive Li | LiNi0.5Mn1.5O4 cell at 5.0 V with excellent capacity retention further reveals the effectiveness of q‐LHCE. The refinement of old‐fashioned carbonate electrolytes provides new perspectives toward practical high‐voltage battery systems.
papergraph.benchmark: cycle life
papergraph.contribution: Development of a quasi-localized high-concentration electrolyte (q-LHCE) that enhances the performance and stability of high-voltage lithium metal batteries.
papergraph.limitation: A practical ampere‐hour level graphite | LiCoO2 pouch cell at 4.5 V and aggressive Li | LiNi0.5Mn1.5O4 cell at 5.0 V with excellent capacity retention further reveals the effectiveness of q‐LHCE.

papergraph.title: Fluorinated ether electrolyte with controlled solvation structure for high voltage lithium metal batteries
papergraph.citation: Yan Zhao et al. (2022) Fluorinated ether electrolyte with controlled solvation structure for high voltage lithium metal batteries doi:10.1038/s41467-022-29199-3
papergraph.abstract: The development of new solvents is imperative in lithium metal batteries due to the incompatibility of conventional carbonate and narrow electrochemical windows of ether-based electrolytes. Whereas the fluorinated ethers showed improved electrochemical stabilities, they can hardly solvate lithium ions. Thus, the challenge in electrolyte chemistry is to combine the high voltage stability of fluorinated ethers with high lithium ion solvation ability of ethers in a single molecule. Herein, we report a new solvent, 2,2-dimethoxy-4-(trifluoromethyl)-1,3-dioxolane (DTDL), combining a cyclic fluorinated ether with a linear ether segment to simultaneously achieve high voltage stability and tune lithium ion solvation ability and structure. High oxidation stability up to 5.5 V, large lithium ion transference number of 0.75 and stable Coulombic efficiency of 99.2% after 500 cycles proved the potential of DTDL in high-voltage lithium metal batteries. Furthermore, 20 μm thick lithium paired LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 full cell incorporating 2 M LiFSI-DTDL electrolyte retained 84% of the original capacity after 200 cycles at 0.5 C. The development of lithium-metal batteries is limited by the low thermodynamic and/or low voltage stability of conventional electrolytes. Here, the authors combined the high voltage stability of fluorinated ethers with high Li+ solvation ability of ethers in a single molecule and realized highly stable lithium-metal batteries.

papergraph.title: Practical Application of All‐Solid‐State Lithium Batteries Based on High‐Voltage Cathodes: Challenges and Progress
papergraph.citation: Xilong Chen et al. (2023) Practical Application of All‐Solid‐State Lithium Batteries Based on High‐Voltage Cathodes: Challenges and Progress doi:10.1002/aenm.202301230
papergraph.abstract: All‐solid‐state lithium batteries (ASSLBs) have become a recent research hotspot because of their excellent safety performance. In order to better reflect their superiority, high‐voltage cathodes should be applied to enhance the energy density of solid batteries to compete with commercial liquid batteries. However, the introduction of high‐voltage cathodes suffers from many problems, such as low electrochemical stability, inferior interface chemical stability between cathode and electrolyte, poor mechanical contact, and gas evolution. These drawbacks significantly influence the battery performance, even causing battery failure and hindering the commercialization of solid‐state batteries. This paper first reviews the above failure mechanisms of high‐voltage cathode‐based ASSLBs from different perspectives. Then, recent advances in solid‐state electrolytes for ASSLBs are summarized, mainly including polymer solid electrolytes, sulfide solid electrolytes, and oxide solid electrolytes. In addition, the influence of the cathode materials is also highly critical, and strategies to improve electrochemical performance are put forward, which can be divided into coating protection, synthesis modification, and structure improvement. Finally, guidelines for the future development of solid‐state batteries are also discussed.

papergraph.title: High‐Voltage and High‐Safety Practical Lithium Batteries with Ethylene Carbonate‐Free Electrolyte
papergraph.citation: Yu Wu et al. (2021) High‐Voltage and High‐Safety Practical Lithium Batteries with Ethylene Carbonate‐Free Electrolyte doi:10.1002/aenm.202102299
papergraph.abstract: Serious safety issues are impeding the widespread adoption of high‐energy lithium‐ion batteries for transportation electrification and large‐scale grid storage. Herein, a triple‐salt ethylene carbonate (EC) free electrolyte for high‐safety and high‐energy pouch‐type LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2|graphite (NMC811|Gr) cells is reported. This EC‐free electrolyte can effectively stabilize the NMC811 surface under high potential (up to 4.5 V), as well as generate a stable interphase to achieve a superior compatibility with the Gr anode. The electrolyte strategy enables significantly enhanced intrinsic safety (trigger temperature of thermal runaway (TR) increased by 67.0 °C), excellent electrochemical properties (4.2V, ≈100% after 200 cycles), and superior high voltage stability (4.5 V, 82.1% after 200 cycles). The work opens up a new avenue for developing novel electrolyte systems to build safer high‐energy batteries for practical applications.

papergraph.title: Ion bridging enables high-voltage polyether electrolytes for quasi-solid-state batteries
papergraph.citation: Tianyi Hou et al. (2025) Ion bridging enables high-voltage polyether electrolytes for quasi-solid-state batteries doi:10.1038/s41467-025-56324-9
papergraph.abstract: Polyether electrolytes have been widely recognized for their favorable compatibility with lithium-metal, yet they are hampered by intrinsically low oxidation thresholds, limiting their potential for realizing high-energy Li-metal batteries. Here, we report a general approach involving the bridge joints between non-lithium metal ions and ethereal oxygen, which significantly enhances the oxidation stability of various polyether electrolyte systems. To demonstrate the feasibility of the ion-bridging strategy, a Zn2+ ion-bridged polyether electrolyte (Zn-IBPE) with an extending electrochemical stability window of over 5 V is prepared, which enables good cyclability in 4.5 V Li||LiCoO2 batteries. Ampere-hour-level quasi-solid-state batteries of SiO-graphite||LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 (10 Ah, N/P ratio of 1.12, 303 Wh kg−1 at 0.1 C based on the total weight of the pouch cells) and 60 μm-Li||LiNi0.9Mn0.05Co0.05O2 (18 Ah, N/P ratio of 2.5, 452 Wh kg−1 at 0.33 C based on the total weight of the pouch cells) pouch cells with Zn-IBPE present elevated electrochemical performance, benefiting from adequate interfacial stability. Nail penetration tests evidence high battery safety enabled by Zn-IBPE in 4 Ah graphite||LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 pouch cells without combustion or smoke. This work offers a pathway for designing high-voltage polymer electrolytes and a general solution for achieving high-performance quasi-solid-state batteries. The application of polyether electrolytes for Li-ion batteries is limited by their poor oxidative stability. Here, authors report an ion-bridging approach to extend their oxidation potential by stabilizing lone pairs of ethereal oxygen through Zn2+−O coordination.

papergraph.title: Dihydrogen-bonding interactions in ether-based electrolytes to enable high-voltage lithium metal batteries
papergraph.citation: Meinan Zhao et al. (2026) Dihydrogen-bonding interactions in ether-based electrolytes to enable high-voltage lithium metal batteries. doi:10.1038/s41467-026-74013-z
papergraph.abstract: High-voltage lithium metal batteries require electrolytes that simultaneously combine oxidative stability with Li metal compatibility, posing a major challenge for conventional ether-based systems, which are typically limited to voltages below 4.0 V. Although conventional electrolyte engineering has been widely employed to enhance oxidative stability, they often compromise ionic conductivity or require complex synthetic routes. Herein, we propose a strategy based on dihydrogen-bonding interactions by introducing 0.05 M LiBH₄ into conventional ether-based electrolytes to construct a dihydrogen-bonded electrolyte. The hydridic H- in BH₄- interacts with the active Hδ⁺ atoms of 1,2-dimethoxyethane to form dihydrogen bonds, thereby weakening the Li⁺-solvent interaction, accelerating Li⁺ de-solvation, and promoting uniform Li deposition. Simultaneously, these dihydrogen-bonding interactions shield the active Hδ⁺ sites of the solvent within the positive electrode interface, thereby significantly suppressing the oxidative decomposition of 1,2-dimethoxyethane. As a result, the oxidative stability of the electrolyte is extended to 5.54 V without compromising ionic conductivity (>16 mS cm-1/30 °C). Lithium metal full cells using this electrolyte exhibit stable cycling at 4.5 V. This study provides a promising pathway for the design of high-voltage ether-based electrolytes.

papergraph.title: Multiscale interfacial stabilization via prelithiation separator engineering for Ah-level anode-free lithium batteries
papergraph.citation: Ahu Shao et al. (2025) Multiscale interfacial stabilization via prelithiation separator engineering for Ah-level anode-free lithium batteries doi:10.1038/s41467-025-59521-8
papergraph.abstract: Anode-free lithium batteries represent a promising avenue for high-energy-density storage, yet their practical application is hindered by lithium inventory loss from parasitic interfacial reactions, cathode degradation, and limited Li+ reversibility. Herein, we propose a polyolefin separator integrated with a Li2S@C sacrificial layer, achieving multiscale interfacial stabilization in Ah-class anode-free pouch cells. This approach simultaneously replenishes the customized Li+ inventory during the formation cycle and establishes the lithium polysulfide-containing cathode interface with high-voltage tolerance (till 4.5 V). Real-time tracking via in-situ electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and transmission-mode operando X-ray diffraction reveals accelerated Li+ diffusion kinetics and stabilized phase evolution in LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 cathode interfaced with Li2S@C|PE prelithiation separator. Consequently, a 1.22 Ah pouch cell with an Ag-modified Cu foil and LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 cathode is assembled with Li2S@C|PE separator and exhibits gravimetric and volumetric energy densities of 450 Wh kg-1 and 1355 Wh L-1, respectively. This prelithiation protocol demonstrates upscaling potential and generic applicability to secure the interfacial chemistries for anode free/less lithium metal batteries. Anode-free lithium batteries are hindered by lithium inventory loss from parasitic interfacial reactions, cathode degradation, and limited Li+ reversibility. Here, authors propose a prelithiation separator engineering to enable multiscale interfacial stabilization in Ah-class anode-free pouch cells.

papergraph.contribution: multiscale interfacial stabilization in Ah-class anode-free pouch cells, upscaling potential, and generic applicability to secure the interfacial chemistries for anode-free/less lithium metal batteries
papergraph.limitation: Herein, we propose a polyolefin separator integrated with a Li2S@C sacrificial layer, achieving multiscale interfacial stabilization in Ah-class anode-free pouch cells.

papergraph.title: Perspectives on Improving the Safety and Sustainability of High Voltage Lithium‐Ion Batteries Through the Electrolyte and Separator Region
papergraph.citation: H. Cavers et al. (2022) Perspectives on Improving the Safety and Sustainability of High Voltage Lithium‐Ion Batteries Through the Electrolyte and Separator Region doi:10.1002/aenm.202200147
papergraph.abstract: Lithium‐ion batteries (LIBs) are promising candidates within the context of the development of novel battery concepts with high energy densities. Batteries with high operating potentials or high voltage (HV) LIBs (>4.2 V vs Li+/Li) can provide high energy densities and are therefore attractive in high‐performance LIBs. However, a variety of challenges (including solid electrolyte interface (SEI), lithium plating, etc.) and related safety issues (such as gas formation or thermal runaway effects) must be solved for the practical, widespread application of HV‐LIBs. Most of these challenges arise in the region between the electrodes: the electrolyte region. This review provides an overview of recent development and progress on the electrolyte region, including liquid electrolytes, ionic liquids, gel polymer electrolytes, separators, and solid electrolytes for HV‐LIBs applications. A focus on improving the safety of these systems, with some perspectives on their relative cost and environmental impact, is given. Overall, the new information is encouraging for the development of HV‐LIBs, and this review serves as a guide for potential strategies to improve their safety, allowing the development of HV‐LIBs, including solid‐state batteries, to be accelerated to practical relevance.

papergraph.title: An Inorganic‐Dominate Molecular Diluent Enables Safe Localized High Concentration Electrolyte for High‐Voltage Lithium‐Metal Batteries
papergraph.citation: Qianqian Liu et al. (2022) An Inorganic‐Dominate Molecular Diluent Enables Safe Localized High Concentration Electrolyte for High‐Voltage Lithium‐Metal Batteries doi:10.1002/adfm.202209725
papergraph.abstract: The development of high‐voltage Lithium‐metal batteries (LMBs) is hindered by suitable electrolytes that are simultaneously compatible with both high‐voltage cathodes and Li anodes. Herein, a novel localized high‐concentration electrolyte with ethoxy(pentafluoro)cyclotriphosphazene (PFPN) as a nonflammable diluent is developed. The inorganic‐dominate diluent improves the safety of the organic electrolyte, and helps to construct robust passivation interphases on both electrodes. Specifically, PFPN accelerates the complete reduction of anions, leading to a stable anion‐derived interphase layer on Li anode. Meanwhile, PFPN and anions co‐participate in the formation of cathode‐electrolyte interphase, suppressing side reactions and the structural damage of high‐voltage Ni‐rich cathodes. As a result, PFPN‐based electrolyte prolongs the cycling life of LMBs based on high‐voltage LiNi0.6Co0.2Mn0.2 (NCM622) and LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1 (NCM811) cathodes. Specially, 25‐µm‐thick Li paired with NCM622 with a N/P ratio of 1.3 (4.4 V) exhibits excellent capacity retention of above 90% after 200 cycles. This study highlights the important role of diluent in tailoring electrode/electrolyte interphases and provides a new strategy for designing high‐safety electrolytes toward high‐voltage LMBs.

papergraph.title: Hydrofluoric Acid‐Removable Additive Optimizing Electrode Electrolyte Interphases with Li+ Conductive Moieties for 4.5 V Lithium Metal Batteries
papergraph.citation: Gaoxue Jiang et al. (2023) Hydrofluoric Acid‐Removable Additive Optimizing Electrode Electrolyte Interphases with Li+ Conductive Moieties for 4.5 V Lithium Metal Batteries doi:10.1002/adfm.202214422
papergraph.abstract: High‐voltage lithium metal batteries (LMBs) are capable to achieve the increasing energy density. However, their cycling life is seriously affected by unstable electrolyte/electrode interfaces and capacity instability at high voltage. Herein, a hydrofluoric acid (HF)‐removable additive is proposed to optimize electrode electrolyte interphases for addressing the above issues. N, N‐dimethyl‐4‐(4,4,5,5‐tetramethyl‐1,3,2‐dioxaborolan‐2‐yl) aniline (DMPATMB) is used as the electrolyte additive to induce PF6− decomposition to form a dense and robust LiF‐rich solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) for suppressing Li dendrite growth. Moreover, DMPATMB can help to form highly Li+ conductive Li3N and LiBO2, which can boost the Li+ transport across SEI and cathode electrolyte interphase (CEI). In addition, DMPATMB can scavenge traced HF in the electrolyte to protect both SEI and CEI from the corrosion. As expected, 4.5 V Li|| LiNi0.6Co0.2Mn0.2O2 batteries with such electrolyte deliver 145 mAh g−1 after 140 cycles at 200 mA g−1. This work provides a novel insight into high‐voltage electrolyte additives for LMBs.

papergraph.title: Sulfur Solutions: Advancing High Voltage and High Energy Lithium Batteries with Organosulfur Electrolytes
papergraph.citation: Michael Dato et al. (2024) Sulfur Solutions: Advancing High Voltage and High Energy Lithium Batteries with Organosulfur Electrolytes doi:10.1002/aenm.202303794
papergraph.abstract: Achieving energy densities exceeding 350 Wh kg−1 while operating at elevated voltages (>4.5 V vs Li/Li+) is attainable through judicious selection of electrochemical pairs at the cathode and anode. However, current state‐of‐the‐art electrolytes exhibit limited stability when exposed to systems operating at or above 4.3 V. This limitation contributes to the degradation of electrode materials and raises critical safety concerns, impeding the commercialization of such systems. Consequently, there has been a notable surge in research efforts aimed at developing innovative electrolyte compositions capable of supporting high‐voltage lithium‐ion batteries (LIBs). A substantial portion of this research has focused on the family of organosulfur molecules, which possess high oxidative stability. Organosulfur salts also facilitate the formation of dense, ionically conductive solid electrolyte interfaces (SEI) and demonstrate excellent solubility. This article provides a comprehensive overview of the field of organosulfur electrolyte components for their applications in energy storage, encompassing solvents, alternative conducting salts, and additives. It emphasizes the idea that the deliberate design of electrolyte compositions is instrumental in controlling electrode passivation, with organosulfur‐based structures historically proving advantageous in every aspect of the electrolyte. Crucially, it should be noted that many of these components are commercially available, holding significant implications for industrial applications.

papergraph.title: Tailoring Stable PEO‐Based Electrolyte/Electrodes Interfaces via Molecular Coordination Regulating Enables 4.5 V Solid‐State Lithium Metal Batteries
papergraph.citation: Chaowei He et al. (2024) Tailoring Stable PEO‐Based Electrolyte/Electrodes Interfaces via Molecular Coordination Regulating Enables 4.5 V Solid‐State Lithium Metal Batteries doi:10.1002/adfm.202410350
papergraph.abstract: Solid‐state lithium metal batteries (SSLMBs) with poly (ethylene oxide) (PEO)‐based electrolytes have increasingly become one of the most promising battery technologies due to high energy density and safety. However, adverse electrode/electrolyte interface compatibility issues hinder further application. Herein, a PEO‐based composite solid electrolyte with excellent anode and cathode interfacial compatibility is designed via the coordination modulation strategy induced by lithium difluorobis(oxalato)phosphate (DFBOP). By utilizing this electrolyte, the robust inorganic‐rich interphase involving LiF, LixPOyFz, and P─O components is in situ generated on lithium (Li) anode and LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 (NCM811) cathode surfaces via forceful coordination among PEO, lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulphonyl)imide, and DFBOP and subsequent adjustment of front orbital energy levels. It contributes to homogeneous lithium deposition and an effective impediment of PEO oxidation decomposition at high voltage, promoting superior interfacial stability. Consequently, Li‐symmetric cells with modified electrolyte can achieve a stable cycle over 7000 h at 0.2 mA cm−2. Specially, the cathode electrolyte interphase with a unique organic–inorganic interpenetration network structure enables the 4.5 V Li/NCM811 cells to cycle steadily over 100 cycles, with a high discharge capacity of 215.4 mAh g−1 and initial coulombic efficiency of 91.23%. This research has shed light on the interfacial design of PEO‐based electrolytes from the perspective of electrolyte coordination regulation to construct high‐performance SSLMBs.
papergraph.scenario: solid-state lithium metal batteries (SSLMBs) with PEO-based electrolytes
papergraph.benchmark: stable cycle duration, discharge capacity, coulombic efficiency
papergraph.contribution: designing a PEO-based composite solid electrolyte that enhances interfacial stability and performance in SSLMBs, enabling 4.5 V operation
papergraph.limitation: However, adverse electrode/electrolyte interface compatibility issues hinder further application.

papergraph.title: Thermal Runaway Inhibition of Lithium‐Ion Batteries Employing Thermal‐Driven Phosphazene Based Electrolytes
papergraph.citation: Weifeng Zhang et al. (2025) Thermal Runaway Inhibition of Lithium‐Ion Batteries Employing Thermal‐Driven Phosphazene Based Electrolytes doi:10.1002/adfm.202508688
papergraph.abstract: Organic electrolyte is a threat to the safe operation for Ni‐rich lithium ion batteries due to its flammability and high voltage cycle instability. Exploring advanced battery electrlytes with high safety and high voltage cyclability is of great significance to the development of electrical vehicles and grid energy storage. Herein, a multi‐functional electrolyte additive, ethoxy‐(pentafluoro)‐cyclotriphosphazene, for high‐safety and high‐energy pouch‐type LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2|graphite (NMC811|Gr) cells is explored. It combined the structure of non‐flammable cyclophosphazene with fluorine, with a good electrochemical compatibility. The high efficiency of the flame retardant produced properties that can not be achieved using “normal” fluorine‐based flame retardants for thermal runaway inhibition. Moreover, the phosphazene (C2H5F5N3OP3)‐based electrolyte (FPEele) endowed an NCM811|Gr pouch cell with extraordinary safety (thermal runaway trigger temperature increased by +41.7 °C, and its highest temperature is decreased by ─205.7 °C) and electrochemical performance (4.5 V high‐voltage cycling, 81.7% capacity retention after 200 cycles). The capacity fading and thermal safety of the battery are simultaneously improved based on the additive engineering. In fact, the phosphazene‐based additive contained F, P, and N atoms, which stabilized the electrode interface and synergistically suppressed combustion during battery failure. Thus, such a work can provide a new ideal for designing a multi‐functional electrolyte.

papergraph.title: Solvation Structure and Derived Interphase Tuning for High-Voltage Ni-Rich Lithium Metal Batteries with High Safety Using Difluorinated Ionic Liquid Based Dual-Salt Electrolytes
papergraph.citation: Yixing Li et al. (2024) Solvation Structure and Derived Interphase Tuning for High-Voltage Ni-Rich Lithium Metal Batteries with High Safety Using Difluorinated Ionic Liquid Based Dual-Salt Electrolytes. doi:10.1002/anie.202317148
papergraph.abstract: Stabilizing electrolytes for high-voltage lithium metal batteries (LMBs) is crucial yet challenging, as they need to ensure stability against both Li anodes and high-voltage cathodes (above 4.5V versus Li/Li+), addressing issues like poor cycling and thermal runaway. Herein, a novel gem-difluorinated skeleton of ionic liquid (IL) is designed and synthesized, and its non-flammable electrolytes successfully overcome aforementioned challenges. By creatively using dual salts, fluorinated ionic liquid and dimethyl carbonate as a co-solvent, the solvation structure of Li+ ions is efficiently controlled through electrostatic and weak interactions that are well unveiled and illuminated via nuclear magnetic resonance spectra. The as-prepared electrolytes exhibit high security avoiding thermal runaway and show excellent compatibility with high-voltage cathodes. Besides, the solvation structure derives a robust and stable F-rich interphase, resulting in high reversibility and Li-dendrite prevention. LiNi0.6Co0.2Mn0.2O2/Li LMBs (4.5 V) demonstrate excellent long-term stability with a high average Coulombic efficiency (CE) of at least 99.99% and a good capacity retention of 90.4% over 300 cycles, even can work at a higher voltage of 4.7 V. Furthermore, the ultrahigh Ni-rich LiNi0.88Co0.09Mn0.03O2/Li system also delivers excellent electrochemical performance, highlighting the significance of fluorinated IL-based electrolyte design and enhanced interphasial chemistry in improving battery performance.

keynotes.title: High-Concentration Ether Electrolytes for Stable High-Voltage Lithium Metal Batteries

keynote.title: High-Concentration Ether Electrolytes for Stable High-Voltage Lithium Metal Batteries
keynote.core_problem: The high reactivity of lithium (Li) metal with traditional electrolytes in high-voltage (>4.3 V) rechargeable lithium metal batteries (LMBs) and the limited oxidation stability of conventional ether-based electrolytes at voltages above 4.0 V.
contributions: Development of high-concentration ether electrolytes that form a unique cathode electrolyte interphase, enhancing the stability of LMBs under high voltages.
methods: Utilizing the synergy between the salt and ether solvent to induce the formation of a stable interphase at the cathode.
experiments_or_evidence: LMBs with the new electrolytes retained 92% capacity after 500 cycles at 4.3 V with minimal Li consumption.
Demonstrated stable battery cycling at voltages up to 4.5 V and on Ni-rich layered cathodes.
assumptions: The unique cathode electrolyte interphase formed by the high-concentration ether electrolytes is effective in stabilizing the cathodes and preserving their structural integrity under high voltages.

gap_signals.text: These findings significantly expand knowledge of ether electrolytes and provide new perspectives of electrolyte design for high-voltage LMBs.

important_claims.claim: High-concentration ether electrolytes can stabilize LMBs at high voltages.
important_claims.evidence: LMBs with the new electrolytes retained 92% capacity after 500 cycles at 4.3 V with very limited Li consumption.

important_claims.claim: The new electrolytes enable stable battery cycling at voltages as high as 4.5 V and on Ni-rich layered cathodes.
important_claims.evidence: Stable battery cycling was observed not only under voltages as high as 4.5 V but also on highly demanding Ni-rich layered cathodes.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The study provides a novel approach to designing electrolytes for high-voltage LMBs, which can be further explored and optimized for various high-performance battery applications.

keynotes.title: Molecular anchoring of free solvents for high-voltage and high-safety lithium metal batteries

keynote.title: Molecular anchoring of free solvents for high-voltage and high-safety lithium metal batteries
keynote.core_problem: Constraining the electrochemical reactivity of free solvent molecules in ether-based electrolytes to improve the performance and safety of high-voltage lithium metal batteries.
contributions: Proposed a molecular anchoring approach to restrict the interfacial reactivity of free ether solvents in diluted electrolytes, enhancing the stability and safety of lithium metal batteries.
methods: Utilized hydrogen-bonding interactions from an anchoring solvent to suppress excessive ether side reactions and enhance the stability of nickel-rich cathodes at high voltages (4.7 V).

gap_signals.text: The typical high concentration electrolyte approach faces severe side reactions under high voltages and extensive exothermic reactions between Li metal and reactive anions, which this new approach aims to mitigate.

important_claims.claim: The molecular anchoring approach enhances the stability of nickel-rich cathodes at 4.7 V, despite the extremely low Li+/ether molar ratio (1:9) and the absence of typical anion-derived interphase.
The exothermic processes under thermal abuse conditions are mitigated due to the reduced reactivity of anions, effectively postponing the battery thermal runaway.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This study provides a novel method for improving the electrochemical and thermal stabilities of lithium metal batteries, which can be further explored and adapted in other battery systems or with different materials to achieve similar benefits.

keynotes.title: Lithium hexamethyldisilazide as electrolyte additive for efficient cycling of high-voltage non-aqueous lithium metal batteries

keynote.title: Lithium hexamethyldisilazide as electrolyte additive for efficient cycling of high-voltage non-aqueous lithium metal batteries
keynote.core_problem: High-voltage lithium metal batteries suffer from poor cycling stability due to the detrimental effect of water moisture and impurities in the non-aqueous liquid electrolyte, especially at high operating temperatures (≥60 °C).
contributions: Introduction of lithium hexamethyldisilazide (LiHMDS) as an electrolyte additive to improve cycling stability.
methods: Addition of 0.6 wt% LiHMDS to a fluorine-containing carbonate-based non-aqueous electrolyte solution.
Evaluation of Li||NCM811 coin cell performance up to 1000 or 500 cycles with a high cut-off voltage of 4.5 V in the temperature range of 25 °C−60 °C.
experiments_or_evidence: Stable operation of Li||NCM811 coin cells up to 1000 or 500 cycles with a high cut-off voltage of 4.5 V in the 25 °C−60 °C temperature range.
assumptions: The presence of hydrofluoric acid (HF) and water in the electrolyte solution is detrimental to the battery's cycling stability.

gap_signals.text: Further research could explore the long-term stability and safety of LiHMDS in different battery chemistries and under varying conditions.

important_claims.claim: LiHMDS acts as a scavenger for hydrofluoric acid and water, facilitating the formation of a robust cathode|electrolyte interphase (CEI).
important_claims.evidence: The addition of LiHMDS enables stable Li||NCM811 coin cell operation up to 1000 or 500 cycles with a high cut-off voltage of 4.5 V in the 25 °C−60 °C temperature range.

important_claims.claim: The LiHMDS-derived CEI prevents Ni dissolution, mitigates irreversible phase transformation, and suppresses side reactions with the electrolyte solution.
important_claims.evidence: The stable operation of the Li||NCM811 coin cells over multiple cycles indicates the effectiveness of the CEI in protecting the cathode and reducing side reactions.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This study provides a promising approach for improving the cycling stability of high-voltage lithium metal batteries by using LiHMDS as an electrolyte additive, which can be further investigated and applied in other battery systems.

keynotes.title: Enabling High-Voltage Lithium-Metal Batteries under Practical Conditions

keynote.title: Enabling High-Voltage Lithium-Metal Batteries under Practical Conditions
keynote.core_problem: Developing high-energy-density rechargeable lithium-metal batteries (LMBs) that can operate under practical conditions, including high voltage, high-loading cathode, thin Li anode, and lean electrolyte, has been challenging due to stability issues.
contributions: Reported an ether-based localized high-concentration electrolyte that enhances the stability of a Ni-rich NMC811 cathode under 4.4 and 4.5 V.
Demonstrated improved cycling performance of Li||NMC811 batteries with limited amounts of Li anode and electrolyte.
methods: Development and use of an ether-based localized high-concentration electrolyte
Formation of a protective interphase enriched in LiF on the NMC811 cathode
experiments_or_evidence: Cycling performance tests showing over 80% capacity retention in 150 cycles under highly challenging conditions
assumptions: The ether-based electrolyte is effective in enhancing the stability of the NMC811 cathode at high voltages.

gap_signals.text: The findings point out a promising strategy for developing practical high-energy LMBs, suggesting further research into optimizing the electrolyte and battery design.

important_claims.claim: The ether-based localized high-concentration electrolyte greatly enhances the stability of the NMC811 cathode under 4.4 and 4.5 V.
important_claims.evidence: Cycling performance tests show over 80% capacity retention in 150 stable cycles with extremely limited amounts of the Li anode and electrolyte.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This work provides a new approach to stabilizing high-voltage LMBs, which can be built upon by researchers to further improve battery performance and develop more practical high-energy-density battery systems.

keynotes.title: Dynamic doping and interphase stabilization for cobalt-free and high-voltage Lithium metal batteries

keynote.title: Dynamic doping and interphase stabilization for cobalt-free and high-voltage Lithium metal batteries
keynote.core_problem: Cobalt-free spinel LiNi0.5Mn1.5O4 (LNMO) positive electrodes suffer from Mn dissolution, electrolyte decomposition at high voltage, and lithium dendrite growth, which compromise cycling stability.
contributions: Introduction of ferrocene hexafluorophosphate as an electrolyte additive to achieve dynamic doping of Fe3+ in positive electrodes during electrochemical cycling
Formation of a thin, dense inorganic positive electrode electrolyte interphase and F, P-rich inorganic solid electrolyte interphase, stabilizing both the positive and negative electrode interfaces
Enhancement of cycling stability and rate performance in Li || LNMO batteries at a charge cutoff voltage of 4.9 V
Demonstration of the universal applicability of ferrocene hexafluorophosphate in stabilizing positive electrodes operating at challenging voltages, including other lithium-ion battery chemistries
methods: Electrochemical cycling with ferrocene hexafluorophosphate as an additive in the electrolyte
Analysis of interphase formation on both positive and negative electrodes
experiments_or_evidence: Li || LNMO batteries with modified electrolytes show enhanced cycling stability and rate performance over 160 cycles at 4.9 V charge cutoff voltage
Successful demonstration of a 470 Wh kg−1 level Li metal pouch cell
Application of the additive in stabilizing other high-voltage positive electrodes such as LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2, LiNi0.6Co0.2Mn0.2O2, and LiCoO2

gap_signals.text: Further investigation into the long-term effects and broader application of the ferrocene hexafluorophosphate additive in different battery systems

datasets_or_materials: Ferrocene hexafluorophosphate as an electrolyte additive
Other high-voltage positive electrodes: LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2, LiNi0.6Co0.2Mn0.2O2, and LiCoO2

important_claims.claim: Ferrocene hexafluorophosphate as an electrolyte additive can dynamically dope Fe3+ in positive electrodes during electrochemical cycling, enhancing stability and performance.
important_claims.evidence: Li || LNMO batteries based on modified electrolytes show consistent performance over 160 cycles and a 470 Wh kg−1 level Li metal pouch cell was successfully realized.

important_claims.claim: The additive forms stable inorganic interphases on both positive and negative electrodes, improving overall battery performance.
important_claims.evidence: Thin, dense inorganic positive electrode electrolyte interphase and F, P-rich inorganic solid electrolyte interphase are formed, effectively stabilizing the electrode interfaces.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The proposed method of using ferrocene hexafluorophosphate as an electrolyte additive can be further explored for other high-voltage battery systems, providing a pathway to enhance their stability and performance.

keynotes.title: In Situ Catalytic Polymerization of a Highly Homogeneous PDOL Composite Electrolyte for Long‐Cycle High‐Voltage Solid‐State Lithium Batteries

keynote.title: In Situ Catalytic Polymerization of a Highly Homogeneous PDOL Composite Electrolyte for Long‐Cycle High‐Voltage Solid‐State Lithium Batteries
keynote.core_problem: Developing solid electrolytes with high ionic conductivity and stability at high voltages for use in solid-state lithium batteries.
contributions: Development of a highly homogeneous poly(1,3-dioxolane) (PDOL) composite solid electrolyte (CSE) membrane through in situ catalytic polymerization using yttria stabilized zirconia (YSZ) nanoparticles.
methods: In situ catalytic polymerization of 1,3-dioxolane (DOL) monomers using YSZ nanoparticles as a catalyst.
experiments_or_evidence: Polymerization conversion of DOL monomers up to 98.5%.
Electrochemical window exceeding 4.9 V.
Room temperature ionic conductivity of 2.75 × 10−4 S cm−1.
Enhanced cycle life of lithium metal anode.
Long cycle life over 800 cycles for NCM622-based solid-state lithium battery.
assumptions: The dispersion of YSZ nanoparticles is uniform and effective in catalyzing the polymerization of DOL.

gap_signals.text: Further enhancement of ionic conductivity and cycle life could be explored.

important_claims.claim: The CSE membrane has a high polymerization conversion of DOL monomers (up to 98.5%) and an electrochemical window exceeding 4.9 V.
important_claims.evidence: Reported results from the experimental setup.

important_claims.claim: The room temperature ionic conductivity of the CSE is 2.75 × 10−4 S cm−1, and it enhances the cycle life of the lithium metal anode.
important_claims.evidence: Experimental measurements and performance testing.

important_claims.claim: The NCM622-based solid-state lithium battery shows a long cycle life over 800 cycles.
important_claims.evidence: Battery performance testing under specified conditions.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The method of in situ catalytic polymerization and the resulting properties of the PDOL CSE can be valuable for further research into developing high-performance solid-state lithium batteries, particularly focusing on improving ionic conductivity and cycle life.

keynotes.title: Non-corrosive asymmetric fluorinated aryl sulfonimide lithium salt for high-temperature and high-voltage lithium metal batteries

keynote.title: Non-corrosive asymmetric fluorinated aryl sulfonimide lithium salt for high-temperature and high-voltage lithium metal batteries
keynote.core_problem: Electrolyte for high-temperature and high-voltage lithium metal batteries face challenges of thermally decomposition of lithium salt and interfacial corrosiveness with aluminum current collectors/cathode materials.
contributions: Development of a non-corrosive asymmetric lithium salt, LiFAS, for high-temperature and high-voltage applications.
methods: Molecular engineering of the lithium salt to include a fluorinated aryl substituent on the bis-sulfonylimide anion
experiments_or_evidence: Thermal stability (decomposition temperature ~388 °C)
High voltage tolerance (anodic decomposition potential ~5.5 V vs. Li/Li+)
High Li+ transference number of 0.62
Formation of a dense Al(FAS)3/AlF3 passivation layer on the surface of Al current collector
Promotion of inorganic-rich interphases on the cathode and anode
Cycling stability and capacity retention in Li||NCM811 full cells (81% after 230 cycles at 60 °C and 4.5 V, 0.5 C charge/1 C discharge)

gap_signals.text: This work inspires molecular engineering strategy for designing functional lithium salts to enhance the cycle life of LMBs under high-temperatures and high-voltages.

important_claims.claim: LiFAS exhibits high thermal stability, high voltage tolerance, and a high Li+ transference number.
important_claims.evidence: Thermal stability (decomposition temperature ~388 °C), high voltage tolerance (anodic decomposition potential ~5.5 V vs. Li/Li+), and a high Li+ transference number of 0.62.

important_claims.claim: LiFAS inhibits Al-corrosion by forming a dense passivation layer.
important_claims.evidence: Formation of a dense Al(FAS)3/AlF3 passivation layer on the surface of Al current collector.

important_claims.claim: LiFAS promotes the formation of inorganic-rich interphases on the cathode and anode.
important_claims.evidence: Promotion of inorganic-rich interphases on the cathode and anode.

important_claims.claim: LiFAS endows Li||NCM811 full cells with great cycling stability and capacity retention under harsh conditions.
important_claims.evidence: Capacity retention of 81% after 230 cycles at 60 °C and 4.5 V, 0.5 C charge/1 C discharge.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The development and testing of LiFAS provide a template for future research in the design of stable and efficient electrolytes for high-temperature and high-voltage lithium metal batteries, particularly focusing on the use of molecular engineering to address interface stability and corrosion issues.

keynotes.title: Advanced Electrolytes for Fast‐Charging High‐Voltage Lithium‐Ion Batteries in Wide‐Temperature Range

keynote.title: Advanced Electrolytes for Fast‐Charging High‐Voltage Lithium‐Ion Batteries in Wide‐Temperature Range
keynote.core_problem: Severe capacity fading of NMC cathode materials with high Ni content (≥0.8) under high charge voltages (e.g., 4.4 V and above) due to unstable cathode/electrolyte interfaces.
contributions: Development of an advanced electrolyte with a high oxidation potential over 4.9 V.
Achievement of excellent cycling stability, good rate capabilities, and superior low-temperature performance in NMC811-based LIBs.
experiments_or_evidence: Demonstration of cycling stability in 2.5–4.4 V at room temperature and 60 °C.
Good rate capabilities under fast charging and discharging up to 3C rate (1C = 2.8 mA cm−2).
Superior low-temperature discharge performance down to −30 °C with a capacity retention of 85.6% at C/5 rate.
assumptions: The electrode/electrolyte interfaces, not the electrolyte conductivity and viscosity, govern the LIB performance.

gap_signals.text: This work sheds light on a very promising strategy to develop new electrolytes for fast-charging high-energy LIBs in a wide-temperature range.

important_claims.claim: The developed advanced electrolyte enables NMC811-based LIBs to achieve excellent cycling stability, good rate capabilities, and superior low-temperature performance.
important_claims.evidence: Demonstrated through experiments showing stable performance in various temperature conditions and charge/discharge rates.

important_claims.claim: Electrode/electrolyte interfaces are more critical than electrolyte conductivity and viscosity for LIB performance.
important_claims.evidence: Performance improvements were observed, focusing on the interface rather than the physical properties of the electrolyte.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The study provides a foundation for developing new electrolytes that can enhance the performance of high-Ni content NMC cathodes in lithium-ion batteries, particularly under high-voltage and wide-temperature conditions.

keynotes.title: Quasi‐Localized High‐Concentration Electrolytes for High‐Voltage Lithium Metal Batteries

keynote.title: Quasi‐Localized High‐Concentration Electrolytes for High‐Voltage Lithium Metal Batteries
keynote.core_problem: Poor compatibility of commercial ester-based electrolytes with Li metal and their instability under high voltage, limiting their use in high-voltage lithium metal batteries.
contributions: Introduction of a quasi-localized high-concentration electrolyte (q-LHCE) that enhances the stability and performance of high-voltage lithium metal batteries.
methods: Partial replacement of solvents in conventional LiPF6 based carbonated electrolyte with fluorinated analogs (FEC, FEMC) that have weak solvating ability.
experiments_or_evidence: Formation of an anion-rich solvation sheath leading to inorganic-dominated solid electrolyte interphases on both cathode and anode.
Uniform Li deposition, suppressed electrolyte decomposition, and cathode deterioration observed with q-LHCE.
Stable cycling of Li | LiCoO2 cells at 4.5 V across a wide temperature range (-20 to 45 °C).
A practical ampere-hour level graphite | LiCoO2 pouch cell at 4.5 V and an aggressive Li | LiNi0.5Mn1.5O4 cell at 5.0 V showing excellent capacity retention.

gap_signals.text: The refinement of old-fashioned carbonate electrolytes provides new perspectives toward practical high-voltage battery systems.

important_claims.claim: q-LHCE supports stable cycling of Li | LiCoO2 cells at 4.5 V across a wide temperature range (-20 to 45 °C) with limited Li consumption.
important_claims.evidence: Experimental results show uniform Li deposition, suppressed electrolyte decomposition, and cathode deterioration.

important_claims.claim: A practical ampere-hour level graphite | LiCoO2 pouch cell at 4.5 V and an aggressive Li | LiNi0.5Mn1.5O4 cell at 5.0 V exhibit excellent capacity retention.
important_claims.evidence: Capacity retention data from the experiments.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The approach of using q-LHCE can be applied to other types of high-voltage lithium metal batteries, providing a method to improve the stability and performance of these systems. The specific combination of fluorinated solvents and the resulting anion-rich solvation sheath can serve as a model for further research into high-voltage electrolytes.

keynotes.title: Fluorinated ether electrolyte with controlled solvation structure for high voltage lithium metal batteries

keynote.title: Fluorinated ether electrolyte with controlled solvation structure for high voltage lithium metal batteries
keynote.core_problem: The incompatibility of conventional carbonate and the narrow electrochemical windows of ether-based electrolytes in lithium metal batteries, combined with the difficulty of achieving both high voltage stability and high lithium ion solvation ability in a single molecule.
contributions: Development of a new solvent, 2,2-dimethoxy-4-(trifluoromethyl)-1,3-dioxolane (DTDL), which combines a cyclic fluorinated ether with a linear ether segment to achieve high voltage stability and tune lithium ion solvation ability.
methods: Synthesis and characterization of DTDL as a new solvent for lithium metal batteries.
experiments_or_evidence: High oxidation stability up to 5.5 V observed with DTDL.
Large lithium ion transference number of 0.75 achieved with DTDL.
Stable Coulombic efficiency of 99.2% after 500 cycles using DTDL.
20 μm thick lithium paired LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 full cell incorporating 2 M LiFSI-DTDL electrolyte retained 84% of the original capacity after 200 cycles at 0.5 C.
assumptions: The combination of a cyclic fluorinated ether with a linear ether segment can provide both high voltage stability and effective lithium ion solvation.
limitations: Long-term performance and scalability of the new solvent in commercial battery applications are not fully explored.

gap_signals.text: Long-term performance and scalability of the new solvent in commercial battery applications are not fully explored.
The long-term performance and scalability of the new solvent in commercial battery applications need further investigation.

important_claims.claim: DTDL provides high voltage stability and effective lithium ion solvation.
important_claims.evidence: High oxidation stability up to 5.5 V, large lithium ion transference number of 0.75, and stable Coulombic efficiency of 99.2% after 500 cycles.

important_claims.claim: Lithium-metal batteries with DTDL show promising performance in full-cell tests.
important_claims.evidence: 20 μm thick lithium paired LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 full cell incorporating 2 M LiFSI-DTDL electrolyte retained 84% of the original capacity after 200 cycles at 0.5 C.

keynotes.title: Practical Application of All‐Solid‐State Lithium Batteries Based on High‐Voltage Cathodes: Challenges and Progress

keynote.title: Practical Application of All‐Solid‐State Lithium Batteries Based on High‐Voltage Cathodes: Challenges and Progress
keynote.core_problem: The introduction of high-voltage cathodes in all-solid-state lithium batteries (ASSLBs) faces challenges such as low electrochemical stability, inferior interface chemical stability between the cathode and electrolyte, poor mechanical contact, and gas evolution, which hinder the commercialization of ASSLBs.
contributions: Review of failure mechanisms of high-voltage cathode-based ASSLBs
Summary of recent advances in solid-state electrolytes for ASSLBs, including polymer, sulfide, and oxide solid electrolytes
Discussion of strategies to improve the electrochemical performance of cathode materials, including coating protection, synthesis modification, and structure improvement
Guidelines for the future development of solid-state batteries

important_claims.claim: Strategies like coating protection, synthesis modification, and structure improvement can enhance the electrochemical performance of cathode materials in ASSLBs.
important_claims.evidence: The paper discusses these strategies as potential solutions to the problems faced by high-voltage cathode-based ASSLBs.

keynotes.title: High‐Voltage and High‐Safety Practical Lithium Batteries with Ethylene Carbonate‐Free Electrolyte

keynote.core_problem: Serious safety issues are impeding the widespread adoption of high-energy lithium-ion batteries for transportation electrification and large-scale grid storage.

methods: Utilization of an EC-free electrolyte to stabilize the NMC811 surface under high potential and generate a stable interphase compatible with the graphite anode.
experiments_or_evidence: Enhanced intrinsic safety with the trigger temperature of thermal runaway increased by 67.0 °C.
Excellent electrochemical properties, retaining ≈100% capacity after 200 cycles at 4.2V.
Superior high voltage stability, retaining 82.1% capacity after 200 cycles at 4.5 V.

important_claims.evidence: Experiments showed that the battery retained 82.1% capacity after 200 cycles at 4.5 V.

keynotes.title: Ion bridging enables high-voltage polyether electrolytes for quasi-solid-state batteries

keynote.title: Ion bridging enables high-voltage polyether electrolytes for quasi-solid-state batteries
keynote.core_problem: Polyether electrolytes have poor oxidative stability, limiting their use in high-energy Li-metal batteries.
contributions: A general approach using ion-bridging to enhance the oxidation stability of polyether electrolyte systems.
Development of a Zn2+ ion-bridged polyether electrolyte (Zn-IBPE) with an extended electrochemical stability window over 5V.
methods: Bridge joints between non-lithium metal ions and ethereal oxygen to stabilize lone pairs of ethereal oxygen through Zn2+−O coordination.
experiments_or_evidence: Demonstration of good cyclability in 4.5V Li||LiCoO2 batteries with Zn-IBPE.
Elevated electrochemical performance in SiO-graphite||LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 and 60 μm-Li||LiNi0.9Mn0.05Co0.05O2 pouch cells with Zn-IBPE.
Nail penetration tests showing high battery safety in 4 Ah graphite||LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 pouch cells without combustion or smoke.
assumptions: The ion-bridging strategy is effective in enhancing the oxidation stability of polyether electrolytes.
limitations: The specific long-term stability and degradation mechanisms of Zn-IBPE are not fully detailed.

gap_signals.text: The specific long-term stability and degradation mechanisms of Zn-IBPE are not fully detailed.
Further investigation into the long-term stability and degradation mechanisms of Zn-IBPE.

60 μm-Li||LiNi0.9Mn0.05Co0.05O2 (18 Ah, N/P ratio of 2.5, 452 Wh kg−1 at 0.33 C)

keynotes.title: Dihydrogen-bonding interactions in ether-based electrolytes to enable high-voltage lithium metal batteries

keynote.title: Dihydrogen-bonding interactions in ether-based electrolytes to enable high-voltage lithium metal batteries
keynote.core_problem: High-voltage lithium metal batteries require electrolytes that simultaneously combine oxidative stability with Li metal compatibility, which is a major challenge for conventional ether-based systems limited to voltages below 4.0 V.
contributions: Proposed a strategy based on dihydrogen-bonding interactions by introducing 0.05 M LiBH₄ into conventional ether-based electrolytes to construct a dihydrogen-bonded electrolyte.
methods: Introducing 0.05 M LiBH₄ into 1,2-dimethoxyethane to form dihydrogen bonds
experiments_or_evidence: The hydridic H- in BH₄- interacts with the active Hδ⁺ atoms of 1,2-dimethoxyethane to form dihydrogen bonds, weakening the Li⁺-solvent interaction and accelerating Li⁺ de-solvation.
These dihydrogen-bonding interactions shield the active Hδ⁺ sites of the solvent within the positive electrode interface, suppressing the oxidative decomposition of 1,2-dimethoxyethane.
The oxidative stability of the electrolyte is extended to 5.54 V without compromising ionic conductivity (>16 mS cm-1/30 °C).
Lithium metal full cells using this electrolyte exhibit stable cycling at 4.5 V.
assumptions: The introduction of 0.05 M LiBH₄ into the electrolyte will form dihydrogen bonds and improve the properties of the electrolyte.

gap_signals.text: This study provides a promising pathway for the design of high-voltage ether-based electrolytes, suggesting further research in this area.

important_claims.claim: The proposed dihydrogen-bonded electrolyte extends the oxidative stability to 5.54 V without compromising ionic conductivity.
important_claims.evidence: The oxidative stability of the electrolyte is extended to 5.54 V without compromising ionic conductivity (>16 mS cm-1/30 °C). Lithium metal full cells using this electrolyte exhibit stable cycling at 4.5 V.

important_claims.claim: The dihydrogen-bonding interactions promote uniform Li deposition and suppress the oxidative decomposition of 1,2-dimethoxyethane.
important_claims.evidence: The hydridic H- in BH₄- interacts with the active Hδ⁺ atoms of 1,2-dimethoxyethane to form dihydrogen bonds, thereby weakening the Li⁺-solvent interaction, accelerating Li⁺ de-solvation, and promoting uniform Li deposition. Simultaneously, these dihydrogen-bonding interactions shield the active Hδ⁺ sites of the solvent within the positive electrode interface, thereby significantly suppressing the oxidative decomposition of 1,2-dimethoxyethane.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The findings provide a new approach for designing high-voltage ether-based electrolytes, which can be valuable for researchers working on improving the performance of lithium metal batteries.

keynotes.title: Multiscale interfacial stabilization via prelithiation separator engineering for Ah-level anode-free lithium batteries

keynote.title: Multiscale interfacial stabilization via prelithiation separator engineering for Ah-level anode-free lithium batteries
keynote.core_problem: Anode-free lithium batteries suffer from lithium inventory loss, cathode degradation, and limited Li+ reversibility, which hinder their practical application.
contributions: Development of a polyolefin separator integrated with a Li2S@C sacrificial layer for multiscale interfacial stabilization in Ah-class anode-free pouch cells.
methods: Integration of a Li2S@C sacrificial layer into a polyolefin separator to replenish Li+ inventory and stabilize the cathode interface.
Use of in-situ electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and transmission-mode operando X-ray diffraction for real-time tracking of Li+ diffusion kinetics and phase evolution.
experiments_or_evidence: A 1.22 Ah pouch cell with Ag-modified Cu foil and LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 cathode assembled with the Li2S@C|PE separator demonstrated gravimetric and volumetric energy densities of 450 Wh kg-1 and 1355 Wh L-1, respectively.
assumptions: The use of a Li2S@C sacrificial layer can effectively replenish the lithium inventory and stabilize the cathode interface, leading to improved battery performance.
limitations: The full text does not provide specific limitations, but potential areas could include the scalability and long-term stability of the proposed separator under various operating conditions.

Further research is needed to confirm the long-term stability and scalability of the prelithiation protocol in different battery configurations and operating conditions.

important_claims.evidence: Real-time tracking via in-situ electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and transmission-mode operando X-ray diffraction revealed accelerated Li+ diffusion kinetics and stabilized phase evolution in the LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 cathode interfaced with the Li2S@C|PE prelithiation separator.

keynotes.title: Perspectives on Improving the Safety and Sustainability of High Voltage Lithium‐Ion Batteries Through the Electrolyte and Separator Region

keynote.title: Perspectives on Improving the Safety and Sustainability of High Voltage Lithium‐Ion Batteries Through the Electrolyte and Separator Region
keynote.core_problem: Improving the safety and sustainability of high voltage (HV) lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) to enable their practical, widespread application. Challenges include solid electrolyte interface (SEI) formation, lithium plating, and related safety issues such as gas formation or thermal runaway effects, which primarily arise in the electrolyte and separator region.
contributions: Provides an overview of recent developments and progress on the electrolyte region for HV-LIBs, including liquid electrolytes, ionic liquids, gel polymer electrolytes, separators, and solid electrolytes.
Focuses on improving the safety of these systems with perspectives on relative cost and environmental impact.

gap_signals.text: A variety of challenges (including SEI, lithium plating, etc.) and related safety issues (such as gas formation or thermal runaway effects) must be solved for the practical, widespread application of HV-LIBs.

important_claims.claim: High voltage LIBs can provide high energy densities and are attractive for high-performance applications.
important_claims.evidence: Batteries with high operating potentials or high voltage (HV) LIBs (>4.2 V vs Li+/Li) can provide high energy densities and are therefore attractive in high‐performance LIBs.

important_claims.claim: The new information is encouraging for the development of HV-LIBs, including solid-state batteries.
important_claims.evidence: Overall, the new information is encouraging for the development of HV-LIBs, and this review serves as a guide for potential strategies to improve their safety, allowing the development of HV-LIBs, including solid‐state batteries, to be accelerated to practical relevance.

Co-participation of PFPN and anions in the formation of the cathode-electrolyte interphase, suppressing side reactions and structural damage of high-voltage Ni-rich cathodes.

keynotes.title: Hydrofluoric Acid‐Removable Additive Optimizing Electrode Electrolyte Interphases with Li+ Conductive Moieties for 4.5 V Lithium Metal Batteries

keynote.title: Hydrofluoric Acid‐Removable Additive Optimizing Electrode Electrolyte Interphases with Li+ Conductive Moieties for 4.5 V Lithium Metal Batteries
keynote.core_problem: Unstable electrolyte/electrode interfaces and capacity instability at high voltage in high-voltage lithium metal batteries (LMBs) affect their cycling life.
contributions: Proposed a hydrofluoric acid (HF)-removable additive to optimize electrode electrolyte interphases in LMBs.
methods: Used N, N-dimethyl-4-(4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-1,3,2-dioxaborolan-2-yl) aniline (DMPATMB) as the electrolyte additive to induce PF6− decomposition and form a dense and robust LiF-rich solid electrolyte interphase (SEI).
Formed highly Li+ conductive Li3N and LiBO2 to boost Li+ transport across SEI and cathode electrolyte interphase (CEI).
Scavenged traced HF in the electrolyte to protect both SEI and CEI from corrosion.
experiments_or_evidence: 4.5 V Li||LiNi0.6Co0.2Mn0.2O2 batteries with the proposed electrolyte delivered 145 mAh g−1 after 140 cycles at 200 mA g−1.

important_claims.claim: DMPATMB can scavenge traced HF in the electrolyte to protect both SEI and CEI from the corrosion.

keynotes.title: Tailoring Stable PEO‐Based Electrolyte/Electrodes Interfaces via Molecular Coordination Regulating Enables 4.5 V Solid‐State Lithium Metal Batteries

keynote.title: Tailoring Stable PEO‐Based Electrolyte/Electrodes Interfaces via Molecular Coordination Regulating Enables 4.5 V Solid‐State Lithium Metal Batteries
keynote.core_problem: Adverse electrode/electrolyte interface compatibility issues in solid-state lithium metal batteries (SSLMBs) with poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO)-based electrolytes hinder their further application.
contributions: Design of a PEO-based composite solid electrolyte with excellent anode and cathode interfacial compatibility using a coordination modulation strategy induced by lithium difluorobis(oxalato)phosphate (DFBOP).
In situ generation of a robust inorganic-rich interphase on the lithium anode and NCM811 cathode surfaces, promoting homogeneous lithium deposition and preventing PEO oxidation at high voltage.
methods: Utilization of DFBOP to induce coordination among PEO, lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulphonyl)imide, and DFBOP for adjusting front orbital energy levels.
Formation of a unique organic–inorganic interpenetration network structure at the cathode electrolyte interphase.
experiments_or_evidence: Li-symmetric cells with the modified electrolyte achieved stable cycling over 7000 hours at 0.2 mA cm−2.
4.5 V Li/NCM811 cells cycled steadily over 100 cycles, with a high discharge capacity of 215.4 mAh g−1 and an initial coulombic efficiency of 91.23%.

datasets_or_materials: NCM811|Gr pouch cells with phosphazene (C2H5F5N3OP3)-based electrolyte (FPEele)

important_claims.claim: The phosphazene-based electrolyte significantly improves the thermal safety and electrochemical performance of NCM811|Gr pouch cells.
important_claims.evidence: Thermal runaway trigger temperature increased by +41.7 °C, and its highest temperature decreased by ─205.7 °C. Additionally, 81.7% capacity retention was achieved after 200 cycles at 4.5 V high-voltage cycling.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This work provides a new approach for designing multi-functional electrolytes that can enhance both the safety and performance of high-energy lithium-ion batteries, which is valuable for further research in advanced battery technologies.

keynotes.title: Solvation Structure and Derived Interphase Tuning for High-Voltage Ni-Rich Lithium Metal Batteries with High Safety Using Difluorinated Ionic Liquid Based Dual-Salt Electrolytes

keynote.title: Solvation Structure and Derived Interphase Tuning for High-Voltage Ni-Rich Lithium Metal Batteries with High Safety Using Difluorinated Ionic Liquid Based Dual-Salt Electrolytes
keynote.core_problem: Stabilizing electrolytes for high-voltage lithium metal batteries (LMBs) to ensure stability against both Li anodes and high-voltage cathodes, addressing issues like poor cycling and thermal runaway.
contributions: Design and synthesis of a novel gem-difluorinated skeleton of ionic liquid (IL)
Development of non-flammable electrolytes using dual salts, fluorinated ionic liquid, and dimethyl carbonate as a co-solvent
Control of the solvation structure of Li+ ions through electrostatic and weak interactions
Creation of a robust and stable F-rich interphase for high reversibility and Li-dendrite prevention
methods: Use of nuclear magnetic resonance spectra to analyze the solvation structure
Electrochemical performance testing of LiNi0.6Co0.2Mn0.2O2/Li LMBs at 4.5 V and 4.7 V
Testing of ultrahigh Ni-rich LiNi0.88Co0.09Mn0.03O2/Li system
experiments_or_evidence: LiNi0.6Co0.2Mn0.2O2/Li LMBs demonstrated a high average Coulombic efficiency (CE) of at least 99.99% and a good capacity retention of 90.4% over 300 cycles
Ultrahigh Ni-rich LiNi0.88Co0.09Mn0.03O2/Li system also showed excellent electrochemical performance

gap_signals.text: Long-term practical applications and scalability of the proposed electrolyte design in commercial battery systems are not discussed.

important_claims.claim: The designed electrolytes exhibit high security, avoiding thermal runaway and showing excellent compatibility with high-voltage cathodes.
important_claims.evidence: The as-prepared electrolytes demonstrated high average Coulombic efficiency and good capacity retention over 300 cycles, even at higher voltages.

important_claims.claim: The solvation structure derived a robust and stable F-rich interphase, resulting in high reversibility and Li-dendrite prevention.
important_claims.evidence: Excellent long-term stability and electrochemical performance were observed in both tested LiNi0.6Co0.2Mn0.2O2/Li and ultrahigh Ni-rich LiNi0.88Co0.09Mn0.03O2/Li systems.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This study provides a detailed approach to designing stable and safe electrolytes for high-voltage lithium metal batteries, which can be used as a reference for future research on improving battery performance and safety.

boxue_delegation_plans.goal: 从高质量文献中识别高电压运行下正极结构退化、界面副反应、氧释放、电解液氧化、容量衰减和安全性之间的机制缺口，生成可验证且可修订的研究假设。
